narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try based on academic and background factors, estimated a 75.2% chance that you would not pass. This high predicted risk of failure automatically triggers a rejection to ensure the program admits students most likely to succeed. The model's decision hinged primarily on your academic decile rankings from earlier years, your undergraduate GPA, and your Year 3 decile, with the latter two standing out as the strongest influences. Most notably, your Year 3 decile ranking of 2 pushed the prediction strongly toward failure, as it signals performance well below the levels typical of students who pass the bar—where the average is around the 6th decile. Your Year 1 decile of 2 also contributed to this risk, though with somewhat less weight, again falling short of the 6th decile average seen among successful bar takers. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA of 3.20 pulled the prediction toward success, coming close to the 3.24 average of those who passed and softening the overall risk somewhat. While your upper-middle family income aligns well with the majority of bar passers (about 45% in that bracket) and your plan to attend full-time matches the norm (over 93% of passers), these did not outweigh the key academic concerns. Your LSAT score of 34, lower than the 37.27 average for passers, played a supporting role but wasn't among the top drivers here. Compared to typical admitted students, who generally rank in higher deciles early on and maintain stronger GPAs, your profile showed more vulnerability in foundational performance metrics that the model weighs heavily for bar success. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background—gender and race weren't factors—but a data-driven call to protect the program's outcomes. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your recent academic record through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and reapplying once you've addressed these core areas, as many students improve and succeed on later attempts. With targeted effort, you can shift your profile closer to that of admitted peers and demonstrate the upward trajectory the model favors.
